5: The Flood narrative (Gen 6-9) has its closest literary parallel in the Epic of Gilgamesh (Tablet XI), in which Utnapishtim survives a flood sent by the gods, builds a boat, releases birds, offers sacrifice on the mountain. Earlier, the Atra-hasis epic provides an even closer parallel to the Genesis flood. The similarities (flood, boat, birds, sacrifice, rainbow-equivalent) are real and have generated both 'common source' theories (shared Mesopotamian memory of a regional catastrophe) and 'polemical adaptation' theories (Israel renarrates the flood with YHWH as the sole sovereign actor, eliminating the capricious divine conflict of the Babylonian originals). Key theological differences: in Genesis the flood has a clear moral rationale (human wickedness, Genesis 6:5) and is followed by a covenant (Genesis 9), not arbitrary divine caprice.
1: The opening of Genesis 6 — "When human beings began to increase in number on the earth and daughters were born to them" — sets the scene for the most contested passage in Genesis. The increase of human population after the Fall is a fulfillment of the creation mandate (Genesis 1:28: "be fruitful and multiply") but in the context of universal wickedness (Genesis 6:5) becomes the precondition for judgment rather than blessing. The ANE flood tradition in the Atra-hasis epic similarly begins with the gods being disturbed by human population growth and noise before deciding to send the flood — Genesis reverses the rationale: the problem is not human noise but human wickedness.
2: The "sons of God" (bĕnê hāʾĕlōhîm) who take human wives is the most debated passage in Genesis. Three main interpretive traditions: (1) Divine beings: bĕnê hāʾĕlōhîm in Job 1:6; Genesis 2:1; 38:7 consistently refers to heavenly beings/angels; the pre-Christian Jewish tradition (1 Enoch 6-11; Jubilees 5; the Dead Sea Scrolls) unanimously identifies them as fallen angelic Watchers; 2 Peter 2:4 and Jude 6 allude to this tradition. (2) Sethite line: the sons of God = righteous descendants of Seth marrying daughters of Cain's line — common in patristic and Reformed interpretation, avoiding supernatural beings. (3) Dynastic rulers: ANE kings routinely claimed divine sonship; the passage may describe tyrant-kings (gibbôrîm, v4) taking multiple wives by coercive power. The divine-being interpretation best fits the word usage elsewhere, but the seriousness of the interpretive debate should not be minimized.
3: YHWH's declaration "My Spirit will not contend with humans forever, for they are mortal; their days will be a hundred and twenty years" is textually difficult. The word translated "contend" (yāḏôn, hapax legomenon) may also mean "abide/remain in." The 120-year limit may refer either to a lifespan limit on humans or to the remaining time before the flood — 120 years as Noah's construction timeline. The Atra-hasis epic similarly records divine deliberations about limiting human lifespan and population before the flood, though by different means. YHWH's declaration here establishes a divine timeline within which Noah will build and warn.
4: The Nephilim (nĕpîlîm) — "those who fall upon" or "the fallen ones" — are described as the offspring of the divine-being/human unions, and as "heroes (gibbôrîm) of old, men of renown (ʾanšê haššēm)." Gibbôr means mighty warrior/hero; ʾanšê haššēm (men of the name/renown) is the language of fame-seeking that Babel will later attempt (Genesis 11:4: "let us make a name for ourselves"). In ANE tradition, semi-divine heroes were a recognized category: Gilgamesh was described as "two-thirds divine, one-third human"; the Akkadian apkallu-sages were semi-divine culture-heroes. Genesis acknowledges the cultural phenomenon without endorsing the mythology — these figures existed, they were mighty, and their existence is connected to the corruption that necessitated the flood.
6: The divine response to universal human wickedness — "The LORD regretted (wayyinnāḥem) that he had made human beings on the earth, and his heart was deeply troubled (wayyitʿaṣṣēḇ)" — uses strongly anthropomorphic language for divine emotion. The word nāḥam (to relent/regret/comfort) can mean both "to be grieved/sorry" and "to relent from a decision." The same root names Noah (Genesis 5:29: "He will comfort/nāḥam us"), creating a wordplay: the one who will bring the post-flood comfort is born into the world that causes YHWH's nāḥam (grief). The ANE flood traditions similarly describe the gods being troubled by humanity (in Atra-hasis, by the noise/clamor); Genesis substitutes a moral rationale for an existential one.
7: The decree to "wipe (ʾemḥeh) from the face of the earth" the entirety of animate creation — humans, animals, birds, ground-creatures — is the un-creation of days 5-6. The same comprehensive scope (kol-ḥayyat hāʾăḏāmāh, every living thing on the ground) that was blessed in Genesis 1:20-28 will be blotted out. The verb māḥāh (to wipe/blot out) is used for wiping out a name from a book (Exodus 32:32-33) and for erasing sin (Psalms 51:9). The flood is presented as a divine erasure of the corrupted creation, preparing for a new beginning — the same structure as the original creation from chaos.
8: The structural pivot: "But Noah found favor (ḥēn) in the eyes of the LORD." Ḥēn (grace/favor) is the quintessential covenant-grace term — it describes a disposition of goodwill that is given, not earned. The same formula recurs for Moses (Exodus 33:12-13), Gideon (Judges 6:17), and others who receive YHWH's particular favor. The structural contrast between vv7 and 8 is stark: universal decree of destruction → one man's grace. This pattern — judgment with one righteous exception — will recur in Sodom (Lot) and Jericho (Rahab). Noah's grace is the narrative hinge on which the flood narrative turns.
9: The three-term description of Noah's character — "righteous (ṣaddîq), blameless (tāmîm) among the people of his time, and he walked faithfully with God (ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm hithallēk)" — is the most comprehensive character commendation in Genesis to this point. Ṣaddîq (righteous) is the covenant-standing term; tāmîm (blameless/wholehearted) is the sacrificial standard applied to unblemished animals (Exodus 12:5; Leviticus 1:3) and to Abraham's called-to-be-blameless standard (Genesis 17:1). "Walked with God" echoes Enoch's description (Genesis 5:22,24) — the pre-Fall garden-communion language. "Among the people of his time" (bĕḏōrōtāyw) introduces a note of comparative evaluation: righteous in his generation — a qualification the rabbinic tradition debated (was Noah righteous relative only to his corrupt contemporaries, or absolutely?)
10: The tôlĕḏôt formula opening (v9: "This is the account of Noah and his family") and the immediate listing of Noah's three sons (Shem, Ham, Japheth) follow the standard genealogical introduction pattern of Genesis. The three sons are named in v10 in an order that does not reflect birth order (Shem is the oldest, but Ham will be addressed first in the moral crisis of Genesis 9:20-25; Japheth is the youngest). The three-son structure mirrors the three-line humanity of the post-flood world: the Table of Nations (Genesis 10) will trace all peoples to one of the three.
11: "The earth was corrupt (tišāḥēt) in God's sight and was full of violence (ḥāmās)." Both terms carry specific legal and prophetic weight. Šāḥat (corrupt/ruin/destroy) is the verbal root for moral and social corruption throughout the prophetic literature (Ezekiel 16:47; Genesis 20:44; Malachi 1:14 — "corrupted animal offering"). Ḥāmās (violence/injustice/wrong) is the specific word for structural social violence throughout the prophets: Amos (Genesis 3:10), Micah (Genesis 6:12), Habakkuk (Genesis 1:2-3; Genesis 2:8,17), and Ezekiel (Genesis 7:11,23; Genesis 8:17) all use ḥāmās for the violence that provokes divine judgment. The pre-flood world is diagnosed in precisely the terms the prophets will use for Israel's eventual exile.
12: "God saw (wayyarʾ) how corrupt the earth had become" — the same verb used in the creation week's "God saw that it was good" (Genesis 1:4,10,12,18,21,25,31) is now used for the inverse perception: God sees the corruption rather than the goodness. The creation-narrative reversal is deliberate: the creation that God saw as "very good" (Genesis 1:31) has been corrupted by "all the people on earth" (kol-bāśār, all flesh). The comprehensive corruption is total: "all flesh had corrupted its way" (kî-hišḥît kol-bāśār ʾet-darkô) — the same verb used in v11's "corrupt," now applied reflexively: flesh corrupted itself.
13: "The earth is filled with violence (ḥāmās) because of them" — YHWH's diagnostic includes a causal statement: the violence fills the earth "because of them" (mippĕnêhem, literally "from before their face/presence"). Human violence is the specific cause of the judgment's comprehensive scope. The decree "I am going to destroy them along with the earth" mirrors the scope of the corruption — what humanity has corrupted, the judgment will erase. The Atra-hasis flood narrative has the gods deciding on the flood after exhausting other plague-and-famine attempts to control human population; Genesis has no such incremental escalation — YHWH's single decree is made on the basis of moral assessment alone.
14: The ark (tēḇāh) is one of only two uses of this word in the OT — the other is Moses's basket-ark in the Nile (Exodus 2:3,5), also coated with pitch. The word differs from the standard word for "ship" or "boat"; it describes a vessel designed for preservation rather than navigation. The pitch coating (kōper) shares its root with kippēr (to cover/atone/make propitiation) — the atonement-covering language of the Levitical sacrificial system. The structural parallel: the ark's pitch-covering protects from the flood waters; the sacrificial covering (kipper) protects from divine judgment. Utnapishtim's boat in the Gilgamesh epic is similarly coated with six measures of pitch, inside and outside.
15: The ark's dimensions — 300 × 50 × 30 cubits — are remarkably similar to the proportions of modern cargo vessels. A cubit of approximately 18 inches gives dimensions of 450 × 75 × 45 feet, or roughly 137 × 23 × 14 meters. The 6:1 length-to-width ratio is the standard barge ratio for stability and capacity in flat-bottomed vessels. The ANE parallels are instructive by contrast: Utnapishtim's ark in the Gilgamesh epic is a perfect cube (60 cubits each side), and the Atra-hasis ark appears circular — both are clearly stylized/symbolic shapes. Genesis's dimensions are the most architecturally realistic of the ancient flood-ark traditions.
16: The ark's structural details — "a roof, leaving below the roof an opening one cubit high all around; put a door in the side… lower, middle and upper decks" — reflect the multi-deck construction of ancient river craft. The "opening one cubit high all around" has been interpreted as a ventilation gap just below the roof — a practical detail consistent with ancient enclosed-vessel construction. The three decks (taḥtiyyim šĕniyyim ûšĕlišiyyim, lower, second, and third) mirror the three-tier ANE cosmological model (underworld, earth, heaven) and the three-compartment tabernacle (outer court, holy place, holy of holies).
17: "Everything on earth will perish" (kōl ʾăšer-bāʾāreṣ yigwāʿ) — the breath-of-life withdrawal is the inverse of Genesis 2:7 (YHWH breathing life into Adam) and Genesis 6:3 (Spirit's animating presence). The flood is an un-creation: the creature who received the breath of life will lose it; the creation that emerged from the watery deep will return to the watery deep. The language of cosmic reversal — heavens, waters, breath of life, land creatures — systematically undoes the creative work of days 2-6. The Noahic covenant of Genesis 9:8-17 will then be a re-creation covenant, establishing that YHWH will never again un-create in this way.
18: The first use of the word "covenant" (bĕrît) in the Bible — "I will establish my covenant (bĕrîtî) with you" — is remarkable for its context: it comes within the decree of universal judgment. The covenant is YHWH's unilateral commitment to Noah before the ark is built, before the flood comes, before the sacrifice is offered. The verb hăqîmōtî (I will establish/confirm) is later used for the formal ratification of covenants already implicit (Genesis 9:9; Genesis 17:7,19,21; Exodus 6:4); it suggests the covenant with Noah is the first explicit articulation of a bond that creation itself implies. The family structure of covenant-inclusion (you, your sons, your wife, your sons' wives) establishes the household as the covenant unit.
19: The command to bring "two of all living creatures" (ûmikkol-haḥay) into the ark to "keep them alive" (lĕhaḥăyôt) is the ecological preservation mandate embedded within the judgment. The animals that YHWH created "good" (Genesis 1:21,25) and blessed with fruitfulness (Genesis 1:22) will be preserved through the judgment, not lost in it. The survival of animal species through the flood establishes the Noahic covenant's scope: it will be made with "every living creature" (Genesis 9:10,12), not only with humanity. The ark is the instrument of creation-preservation, not merely human survival.
20: The animals "will come to you to be kept alive" — the passive construction places the initiative with YHWH, not Noah. Noah does not gather the animals; they come to him. This divine orchestration of the ark-loading is the counterpart to the creation-narrative's "God brought them to Adam" for naming (Genesis 2:19). What YHWH brought to Adam to be named, YHWH now brings to Noah to be preserved — the pattern of divine initiative in animal-human relationships continues through the crisis.
21: The command to store food for the ark's occupants reflects the practical logistics of ancient animal husbandry: extended sea voyages required massive food stores. Archaeological evidence from Bronze Age Egypt and Mesopotamia shows meticulous grain and food storage in large vessels, consistent with the kind of provisioning the ark required. The food-provision command also echoes the creation mandate of Genesis 1:29-30 — both humans and animals were given vegetation for food in the original creation; the ark's food store preserves the same creation-order feeding pattern through the flood.
22: Noah's comprehensive obedience — "Noah did everything just as God commanded him" (wayyaʿaś Nōaḥ kĕkōl ʾăšer ṣiwwāh ʾōtô ʾĕlōhîm kēn ʿāśāh) — is the foundational qualification of the covenant-servant. The same formula describes Moses's execution of the tabernacle instructions (Exodus 39:43; Genesis 40:16) and Joshua's execution of the conquest commands. Comprehensive obedience — "everything (kōl) just as God commanded" — is the mark of the covenant-faithful servant, in contrast to the "every imagination of the heart was only evil all the time" (kol-yēṣer maḥšĕḇôt libbô raq raʿ kol-hayyôm) of the corrupt generation (v5). The contrast between "all evil all the time" and "all things just as commanded" is the narrative's moral bookend.
